search_results.title: What Is On-Page SEO? Key Elements & Best Practices
search_results.snippet: The main goal of on-page SEO is actually pretty simple: To help search engines and users clearly understand exactly what a web page is about.

message.content:
Below is a checklist of **testable, on‑page metadata and content checks** that are explicitly supported by **Google Search Central** documentation, with:

- the **check** (what you would test),
- **what it verifies / why it matters**, and
- the **exact Google Search Central topic name and URL**.

I focus only on requirements or clear best‑practice guidance that can be implemented as automated checks.

---

### 1. `<title>` element – presence

**Check**

- Page contains exactly one non‑empty `<title>` element in the `<head>`.

**What it verifies**

- Confirms there is a **descriptive, topic‑identifying title** for the page, which is a basic signal used to understand the page’s content and to generate search results snippets.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Title link best practices (Use a title that describes your page)**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#title-link`

---

### 2. `<title>` element – descriptive and not generic

**Check**

- `<title>` text is not empty, not just the site/brand name, and not a generic label like “Home” or “Untitled”.
- Contains text that corresponds to the page’s main topic.

**What it verifies**

- Ensures the title is **descriptive and specific**, helping Google and users understand the topic of the page.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Make your title link descriptive**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#write-good-titles`

---

### 3. `<title>` element – reasonable length (not excessively long or stuffed)

**Check**

- `<title>` character length is within a reasonable range (for example 10–70 characters), and:
  - Doesn’t repeat the same or very similar words many times.
  - Doesn’t look like keyword stuffing.

**What it verifies**

- Titles that are **concise and not spammy** are more likely to be used as title links and are less likely to be ignored or rewritten by Google’s systems.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Avoid keyword stuffing in your title link**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#avoid-keyword-stuffing-title`

*(Google doesn’t give an exact character limit, so any exact numeric threshold in a tool is an implementation choice, not a Google requirement.)*

---

### 4. `<title>` element – uniqueness across site

**Check**

- For a given site, the `<title>` of this URL is not identical to the titles of large numbers of other pages (excluding paginated series, etc.).

**What it verifies**

- **Unique, descriptive titles** help Google understand that each page has distinct content and avoid confusing or low‑quality title signals.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Avoid using the same title text across many pages**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#avoid-duplicate-titles`

---

### 5. `<meta name="description">` – presence (optional but recommended)

**Check**

- If present, `<meta name="description">` is in the `<head>` and non‑empty.
- If absent, this is not an error (Google treats it as optional), but tools can flag “missing meta description” as an **optimization opportunity**.

**What it verifies**

- A description meta tag, when present, gives Google a **concise summary of the page** that can be used in search snippets, especially when it matches the user’s query.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Write a compelling, informative meta description**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#meta-descriptions`

---

### 6. `<meta name="description">` – uniqueness

**Check**

- The meta description on this page is not reused verbatim on many other pages of the same site.

**What it verifies**

- **Distinct descriptions** help Google show more relevant snippets and differentiate pages.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Avoid duplicate meta descriptions**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#avoid-duplicate-meta-descriptions`

---

### 7. `<meta name="description">` – appropriate length and informativeness

**Check**

- Description appears to be reasonably concise and readable (for example 50–160 characters as a tool heuristic), and:
  - Summarizes the page’s main content.
  - Avoids keyword stuffing and meaningless boilerplate.

**What it verifies**

- Helps ensure the description is **useful to users**, increasing the chance Google will show it and that users will click.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Write good meta descriptions**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#write-good-meta`

*(Google explicitly avoids hard character limits; any numeric bounds are tool‑level heuristics, not Google rules.)*

---

### 8. Heading structure – presence of a main heading

**Check**

- Page body contains a **clear primary heading** (commonly `<h1>`, though Google does not require a specific level) that represents the main topic.
- At least one heading element (`<h1>`–`<h6>`) is present.

**What it verifies**

- Headings help Google **understand the structure and topical focus** of content.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Use headings to convey content hierarchy**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#headings`

---

### 9. Heading structure – logical hierarchy and non‑abusive use

**Check**

- Headings are used in a **hierarchical order** (e.g., no random jumps from `<h1>` to `<h5>` just for styling).
- Headings are not misused for styling only (e.g., dozens of headings used as small-print styling).
- No obvious keyword stuffing in headings (e.g., repeating the same phrase unnaturally).

**What it verifies**

- A logical heading structure makes content easier for both users and Google to **scan and interpret**, and avoids spammy signals.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Create a logical heading structure**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#use-headings-correctly`

---

### 10. Descriptive link / anchor text – presence of meaningful anchors

**Check**

- Inline links use **descriptive anchor text** indicating the destination or action, not generic text like “click here” when it can be avoided.
- Anchor text is not just a URL.

**What it verifies**

- Google uses anchor text as a **signal about the linked page’s content**, and descriptive anchors also help users and accessibility.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Use descriptive link text**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#link-text`

---

### 11. Descriptive link / anchor text – avoiding over‑optimized or spammy anchors

**Check**

- Anchor text is natural language; it does **not repeatedly stuff exact‑match keywords** in unnatural ways across many internal links.

**What it verifies**

- Reduces risk of appearing manipulative; Google advises against **spammy linking practices** and keyword stuffing in text, including anchors.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Avoid spammy link text**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#avoid-keyword-stuffing`

---

### 12. Image `alt` attributes – presence on informative images

**Check**

- All images that convey information (non‑decorative) have an `alt` attribute present and non‑empty.
- Purely decorative images either:
  - use `alt=""`, or
  - are implemented via CSS (and thus have no `alt`).

**What it verifies**

- Alternative text is used by Google to better understand the image content and is important for **accessibility** and for images to appear in Google Images.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Use alt text for images**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#image-alt-text`

---

### 13. Image `alt` attributes – descriptive, non‑spammy text

**Check**

- `alt` values:
  - Briefly describe the image.
  - Are relevant to the page context.
  - Are not stuffed with keywords (e.g., repeated keyword lists).

**What it verifies**

- Ensures alt text is **helpful and not spam**, which can help image search visibility and overall quality signals.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Write good alt text**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#write-image-alt`

---

### 14. `lang` attribute – presence on `<html>`

**Check**

- `<html>` element includes a `lang` attribute with a valid BCP 47 language code (e.g., `lang="en"`, `lang="fr-CA"`).

**What it verifies**

- Helps Google and assistive technologies understand the **primary language** of the page, important for proper indexing and serving to the right language audience.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Tell Google about your language**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#language`

---

### 15. `lang` attribute – correct, not conflicting with visible content

**Check**

- The declared `lang` matches the actual primary language of the visible content.
- No conflicting `lang` declarations (e.g., `lang="en"` on `<html>` but most content is in another language).

**What it verifies**

- Avoids confusing language signals, helping Google properly **detect and serve language‑appropriate content**.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Provide consistent language information**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#consistent-language`

---

### 16. Character encoding – presence of charset declaration

**Check**

- Document declares a character encoding using one of:
  - `<meta charset="utf-8">` early in `<head>`, or
  - HTTP header `Content-Type` with `charset=UTF-8` (server‑level).
- For HTML5, `<meta charset="utf-8">` should appear near the top of `<head>`.

**What it verifies**

- Ensures text is **decoded correctly**, preventing garbled characters that can interfere with Google’s ability to read and index content.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Use Unicode (UTF-8)**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#charset`

---

### 17. Character encoding – use of UTF‑8

**Check**

- If a charset meta or HTTP header is present, check that the encoding is **UTF‑8** (or at least not an obsolete encoding).
- Flag non‑UTF‑8 encodings as potential issues.

**What it verifies**

- Google recommends **UTF‑8** because it supports all languages and reduces decoding problems across Google systems.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Use UTF‑8 encoding**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#utf-8`

---

### 18. Viewport meta – presence for mobile friendliness

**Check**

- In `<head>`, a viewport meta tag is present, for example:

  ```html
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  ```

- Confirm the page is not using a fixed‑width design that ignores the viewport.

**What it verifies**

- This is a core part of **mobile‑friendly design**, allowing content to scale to different screen sizes; mobile‑friendliness is used in search ranking for mobile.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Configure the viewport for mobile users**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#viewport`

---

### 19. Viewport meta – sensible values, not disabling zoom

**Check**

- `content` attribute uses sensible values such as `width=device-width` and `initial-scale=1`.
- Does not disable user zoom via `user-scalable=no` or excessively restrict it.

**What it verifies**

- Encourages better **user experience on mobile**, which is part of mobile usability evaluation and can affect how Google views the page for mobile search.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Avoid damaging mobile UX with viewport settings**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#viewport-ux`

---

### 20. Core textual content – presence and crawlability

*(Not requested explicitly, but fundamental “content requirement” that can be tested.)*

**Check**

- There is **substantial HTML text content** (not only images or script‑generated text).
- Important content is present in the initial HTML or server‑rendered DOM and visible without requiring blocked resources.
- Check that robots.txt or meta robots do not inadvertently block essential CSS/JS that render content.

**What it verifies**

- Google must be able to **access, render, and index** the main content; thin or non‑crawlable content hinders ranking.[3][4]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Help Google find your content / Make your content easy to access**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#make-it-easy-to-access`

---

### 21. Core textual content – originality and non‑spammy keyword usage

**Check**

- Page has a reasonable amount of original content (not excessively duplicated from other pages on the same site).
- No visible sections with obvious keyword stuffing or hidden text (e.g., same keyword repeated many times, or text hidden via CSS).

**What it verifies**

- Google’s spam policies discourage **duplicate and spammy content**; such pages may rank poorly or be demoted.[3][4]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Avoid creating duplicate or low‑value content**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#avoid-duplicate-content`

---

### 22. Document `<title>` vs. main heading – basic alignment

**Check**

- The `<title>` and main heading (e.g., `<h1>`) share a common main topic or phrase; they are not about completely different topics.

**What it verifies**

- Alignment between title and content supports clearer **topical relevance** and reduces the chance of titles being rewritten by Google.[3]

**Google Search Central topic + URL**

- Topic: **SEO starter guide – Make sure title links match the content**
- URL: `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide#match-title-to-content`

---

If you want, I can next turn this into:

- a **flat CSV or JSON spec** for automated auditing,
- or map each check to **Lighthouse / Page Experience / Search Console** surfaces where relevant.
